mnemonics.title: MLR Assumptions
mnemonics.content: LIONS HN: LINEARRITY, INDEPENDENCE, NORMALITY, HOMOSCEDASTICITY, NO MULTICOLLINEARITY - REMEMBER AS: LINEAR INDEPENDENT OBSERVATIONS NORMALLY HOMOSCEDASTIC WITH NO MULTICOLLINEARITY

mnemonics.title: VIF Interpretation
mnemonics.content: VIF > 10 = BAD (BAD MULTICOLLINEARITY) - REMEMBER: VIF VALUE TEN PLUS MEANS MULTICOLLINEARITY IS SERIOUS

mnemonics.title: Dummy Variable Trap
mnemonics.content: CATEGORICAL VARIABLES WITH K LEVELS NEED K-1 DUMMIES - REMEMBER: ONE CATEGORY ESCAPES AS REFERENCE

acronyms.usage: Method to estimate regression coefficients by minimizing sum of squared residuals
Sum of squared differences between observed and predicted values
Total variance in the dependent variable
Variance explained by the regression model

visualTips.visualization: Imagine each coefficient as a slider: moving X₁ by 1 unit slides Y up or down by β₁ amount while other sliders (X₂, X₃...) stay fixed in position
Think of R² as a score that always rewards adding questions (predictors), while Adjusted R² is a curved score that penalizes adding irrelevant questions to the test
Picture two predictors as parallel arrows pointing in nearly the same direction - hard to tell which one is pushing Y
Imagine a cone widening from left to right in residual plot - errors spread out more as predictions increase
Think of two musicians playing together - the combined effect (interaction) creates a different sound than the sum of their individual performances

analogies.analogy: Like a recipe where multiple ingredients (predictors) combine to determine the final taste (outcome). Each ingredient's coefficient tells you how much that ingredient affects the dish.
Like prices for different features of a car - each feature (predictor) contributes a specific dollar amount to the total price (Y).
Like a scoring system in a competition that penalizes including irrelevant moves - it rewards quality over quantity of predictors.
Like having two doctors giving overlapping diagnoses - hard to know which one's advice actually matters for the patient's recovery.
Like consistent scatter in target practice - the spread of arrows around the bullseye should be similar at all distances.
